tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document for a small media company. Output in markdown. No preamble. No 'Here is...' framing. Start directly with the H1 title. Use Chase Pierson's writing style: blunt, declarative, no filler, no AI-isms, no marketing vocabulary like 'leverage', 'synergy', 'unlock', 'elevate', 'transform', 'journey'. Write the way someone with skin in the game writes — confident, specific, direct.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Big Muddy Inn** — a boutique inn in Natchez, Mississippi with the Blues Room (a working live music venue). Tracy Alderson-Allen is the innkeeper. No restaurant; catering for events comes from third-party partners. Inn is the umbrella brand for Big Muddy Magazine (sub-brand). Both target travelers who'd book a stay (place-based, hospitality-curious).

Cover: voice attributes (3-5), tone (warm but not sentimental, southern but not folksy), do/don't word lists, 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, how the Inn voice differs from the Touring/Records/Radio voice (which is music-industry-facing, not guest-facing), greeting/sign-off conventions, and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell' that any agent or human can read in 30 seconds.

Aim for ~700 words. Use markdown headers, bullets where appropriate. Output the markdown document directly — no surrounding commentary.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document for a small media company. Output in markdown. No preamble. Start directly with the H1 title. Use Chase Pierson's writing style: blunt, declarative, no filler, no AI-isms, no marketing vocabulary. Write the way an editor with strong opinions writes.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Big Muddy Magazine** — sub-brand under Big Muddy Inn. **Editorial brief is locked: target the Inn's potential guest, not the music industry.** The reader is a traveler considering a stay at the Inn — travel-curious, hospitality-curious, soft-music-curious. Tracy Alderson-Allen edits.

Covers: Natchez restaurants, the river, antebellum architecture, the Blues Room as an experience-for-guests, recipes, weekenders, slow-travel angles. Music as a thread, not a topic. Musicians-as-subjects only when their story sells the place.

Does NOT cover: tour reviews, album reviews, music industry news, scene gossip — that's Big Muddy Radio's beat (which is sub-brand of Touring, not Inn). The two content engines never overlap.

Write the voice doc covering: editor's perspective, voice attributes, tone (warm, observational, tactile, slow), do/don't word lists, headline conventions, lede conventions, 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples (especially anti-examples that sound like Pitchfork or industry trade press), and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. Aim for ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, declarative, road-tested writing — the way a touring booker actually talks. No marketing vocabulary.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Big Muddy Touring** — umbrella brand for the music-IP side of the business. Books artists into Mississippi corridor venues, plans routes, on-the-ground promotion. Anchors everything that travels: the band, the records, the radio. Sub-brands: Big Muddy Records and Big Muddy Radio.

Voice should sound like a booker-promoter on the phone: direct, useful, knows the difference between a good night and a bad night, treats artists as professionals not products. Targets: artists, agents, venue owners, promoters — music-industry-facing, NOT guest-facing.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone, vocabulary do/don't lists, 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples (especially anti-examples that sound like a streaming-platform marketing department), how this voice differs from the Inn/Magazine voice (which targets travelers, not industry), email/text conventions when reaching out to a venue or artist, and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct writing — the way a small label that respects its artists actually talks.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Big Muddy Records** — sub-brand under Big Muddy Touring. Three concentric purposes: (1) houses Amy Allen's catalog and Mechanical Bull's catalog, (2) offers non-exclusive promotion partnerships to outside artists who aren't on a label and would benefit from MBT's media structure, (3) for artists approved onto the label proper, adds a low monthly fee for marketing and label-type services (recurring revenue, not just unit sales).

Voice targets: artists deciding whether to work with us, fans buying records, sync agencies looking for music. Music-industry-facing.

The label voice should sound like an artist-friendly indie that has been around — knows the business, doesn't pretend, doesn't oversell, treats artists as collaborators not properties. Skeptical of major-label rhetoric. Honest about what we do and what we don't do.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone, vocabulary do/don't (especially what NOT to sound like — major-label boilerplate, 'breakthrough artist', 'curated talent', etc.), 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, conventions for release announcements vs artist outreach vs fan-facing copy, the audience distinction from Big Muddy Magazine (which targets Inn guests, not music fans), and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, scene-aware writing — the way a host who actually loves the music talks.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Big Muddy Radio** — sub-brand under Big Muddy Touring. Three channels: (1) podcasts + curated playlists distributed to Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube Music; (2) TikTok Live for live talk-radio feel + music programming (TikTok carries the music licensing umbrella); (3) future linear streaming radio when audience justifies. Built around the artists Big Muddy books, documents, and releases. Amy Allen programs.

**Audience: music-first, scene-first, artist-first.** Distinct from Big Muddy Magazine, which targets travelers / Inn guests. Do NOT mix audiences — Magazine = hospitality-curious, Radio = music-curious.

Voice should sound like a host who plays records they actually love, can talk about why a song matters without name-dropping, treats listeners as fellow obsessives. Mississippi corridor regional consciousness without being parochial.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone (warm, knowledgeable, opinionated, never preachy), vocabulary do/don't (especially what NOT to sound like — corporate radio formatting, 'today's hottest', 'all the hits', algorithmic-playlist-speak), 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, conventions for episode descriptions vs playlist blurbs vs TikTok Live promo, the audience distinction from the Magazine, and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document for Chase Pierson Photography. Output in markdown. No preamble. Photographer's first-person voice — direct, observational, tactile.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Chase Pierson Photography** — Chase's editorial and documentary practice. Premium tier, separate rate card from Tuthill Design (which is real estate / pro services). Subjects include musicians, hospitality, architecture (selectively), landscape, aerial. 52,892 photos in the Hetzner Immich archive.

This is a personal practice, not a corporate brand. Voice should sound like a working photographer talks about the work: declarative, technical when it matters, never precious about it. No 'capturing moments', no 'storytelling through imagery'. Just what's in the frame and why.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone (observational, dry, occasional dry humor), vocabulary do/don't (heavy on what NOT to say — Instagram-photographer cliches especially), 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, conventions for project descriptions vs portfolio captions vs commission-pitch emails, how Chase's photo-voice differs from Tuthill Design's photo-voice (premium editorial vs reliable real-estate-grade service), and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document for a real estate photography and design service. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, professional, value-clear writing.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Tuthill Design (DBA)** — real estate media and design service operating in Hudson Valley + Natchez branch. Photo, video, 3D tours, floor plans, LiDAR + AI renderings. Chase Pierson and Elijah Tuthill 50/50 on the parent LLC; team includes Miles. Vicki Wolpert (KW realtor in Athens NY) is a regular customer and the first external multi-tenant MBT platform onboarding.

Voice targets: realtors (primarily), real estate brokers, property developers, the occasional homeowner. B2B, professional, value-clear. NOT premium-editorial (that's Chase Pierson Photography). Reliable-service tier.

Voice should sound like a service business that respects the client's time, knows realtors are juggling 30 listings, makes the value obvious without overselling. Listing descriptions, package pricing, turnaround times, sample shots — concrete and honest.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone (professional, approachable, never salesy), vocabulary do/don't (what NOT to sound like — generic real-estate-marketing cliches, 'showcase your property', 'capture the essence'), 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, conventions for service-page copy vs realtor outreach vs delivery-handoff emails, how the Tuthill voice differs from Studio C voice (sister DBA — production for events/MBT platform), and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are writing a brand voice document for a production company. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, production-floor writing — the way a working production team actually talks.
tasks.prompt:
Write the brand voice document for **Studio C (DBA)** — production for MBT platform work + concert/event production. Bearsville-anchored (Hudson Valley) + Natchez branch opening. Sister DBA under the same Tuthill Design LLC as Tuthill Design. Multicam shoots, broadcast streaming, post-production, premium cinema tier. MBT buys buckets of Studio C hours from the LLC for platform work.

Voice targets: venues, festivals, touring artists, corporate clients (occasional), MBT platform internal stakeholders. B2B, production-savvy, technically literate without being jargon-y for jargon's sake.

Voice should sound like a production team that's done a lot of shows, knows what can go wrong, plans for it, but doesn't sell the planning — just delivers the result. Confident about gear and capabilities without bragging. Honest about what's not in scope.

Cover: voice attributes (5), tone (professional, calm-under-pressure, technical-when-needed), vocabulary do/don't (what NOT to sound like — wedding-videographer cliches, 'cinematic experience', 'cutting-edge equipment'), 5 example sentences in voice + 5 anti-examples, conventions for service-tier descriptions vs venue retainer pitches vs post-production handoffs, how the Studio C voice differs from Tuthill Design voice (real estate / standardized packages), and a one-paragraph 'voice in a nutshell'. ~700 words. Markdown only.

tasks.system: You are an editor pitching article topics for a regional travel/hospitality magazine. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, specific, no marketing-speak. Each topic should sound like a real article a magazine might actually publish.
tasks.prompt:
Generate the first-issue article topic list for **Big Muddy Magazine** — Spring 2026, the inaugural issue under the new editorial brief.

**Editorial brief:** Magazine targets the Inn's potential guest — travelers drawn to Natchez, Mississippi corridor culture, hospitality with music in the mix. NOT music-industry coverage. NOT scene/touring/album-review content (that's Big Muddy Radio's beat). Cover: Natchez restaurants, the river, antebellum architecture, the Blues Room as a guest experience, recipes, weekenders, slow-travel angles, the people who make the place. Music as a thread, not a topic.

Generate 20 article topics, each with:
- Working headline
- 2-sentence treatment (what the article is, what the reader will get from it)
- Estimated word count (range 500-2500)
- Suggested photo angle (1 sentence)
- Why this article makes someone want to book the Inn

Mix article lengths and types: 5 short (500-800w) + 10 medium (1000-1500w) + 5 long-form (2000-2500w). Mix topic types: place pieces, food pieces, people pieces, experience pieces, river/landscape pieces, history pieces, weekender itineraries.

At the end, suggest which 5 should anchor Issue 1 and why.

Markdown only. ~2000 words total.

tasks.system: You are writing customer-facing marketing copy for an indie record label's recurring subscription tiers. Output in markdown. No preamble. Honest, specific, no marketing fluff. Each tier should be obviously different from the next.
tasks.prompt:
Write the customer-facing marketing copy for **Big Muddy Records' recurring monthly label services** — the Tier 3 revenue play (recurring revenue, not just unit sales). Approved artists pay a low monthly fee for label-type services.

Define three tiers (Silver / Gold / Platinum or pick better names):

**Silver** — entry-level monthly subscription for an artist on the label. What's included: distribution to streaming platforms via the Big Muddy Records umbrella, basic monthly stats, social media post templates, listing in the Big Muddy Records artist roster, one quarterly check-in call. Price range: $50-$100/mo.

**Gold** — mid-tier. All Silver, plus: monthly social media management on the artist's behalf, sync-licensing pitch list submission, quarterly press release drafting, inclusion in Big Muddy Radio podcast curation rotation, one annual photo session by Chase Pierson Photography. Price range: $200-$400/mo.

**Platinum** — top tier. All Gold, plus: dedicated A&R-style support, opening slots on Big Muddy Touring corridor dates, full release campaign management for one annual single, sync-licensing exclusive representation, professional video shoot by Studio C, monthly 1:1 strategy call with Amy Allen. Price range: $750-$1500/mo.

For each tier, write:
1. The pitch (1 paragraph, ~80 words) — who this is for and what they get out of it
2. The 'what's included' list (concrete bullets, no fluff)
3. The honest 'what this is NOT' line (1 sentence — sets expectations)
4. Recommended price
5. Sample artist profile this tier fits

Voice: artist-friendly indie label, honest, doesn't oversell, treats artists as collaborators. NO major-label rhetoric. NO 'breakthrough', 'exclusive opportunity', 'industry leader'. Markdown. ~1800 words.

tasks.system: You are writing customer-facing marketing copy for a real estate photography service offering recurring monthly social management subscriptions. Output in markdown. No preamble. Honest, value-clear, realtor-focused.
tasks.prompt:
Write the customer-facing marketing copy for **Tuthill Design's recurring realtor subscription packages** — monthly social media management + ongoing media services for realtors. Built on the MBT platform, this is the Tier 3 recurring revenue play (15-30% to MBT on the recurring portion).

Define three tiers (Essentials / Pro / Premium or pick better names):

**Essentials** — entry-level for solo realtors. What's included: 8 social media posts per month (Instagram + Facebook), monthly listing photo refresh for up to 2 active listings, basic single-listing video (90-second walk-through), branded post templates. Price range: $300-$500/mo.

**Pro** — for realtors with steady inventory (5-15 active listings). All Essentials, plus: 16 social posts/mo, full photo + video shoot per new listing (up to 4 listings/mo), 3D Matterport tour for premium listings, weekly performance report, dedicated content coordinator. Price range: $800-$1200/mo.

**Premium** — for top-producing realtors and small teams. All Pro, plus: unlimited new listings, drone aerial footage, LiDAR floor plans + AI renderings for vacant properties, dedicated production team, weekly content strategy call, lead-generation landing pages per listing. Price range: $2000-$3500/mo.

For each tier, write:
1. The pitch (1 paragraph, ~80 words) — who this is for, what problem it solves, what the realtor gets back in time
2. The 'what's included' list (concrete bullets — counts, dimensions, turnaround times)
3. The honest 'what this is NOT' line (sets expectations, prevents scope creep)
4. Recommended price
5. Sample realtor profile this tier fits (brokerage size, listing volume, market)

Reference Vicki Wolpert (KW realtor, Athens NY, regular Tuthill customer) as the example for the Pro tier somewhere — she's the first external customer on the new MBT multi-tenant platform.

Voice: professional, honest, realtor-respectful (their time is the constraint, not their budget). NO 'showcase your property', NO 'capture the essence', NO 'cinematic experience'. Markdown. ~1800 words.

tasks.system: You are writing customer-facing marketing copy for radio sponsorship packages aimed at small businesses. Output in markdown. No preamble. Concrete, honest, no broadcast-industry boilerplate.
tasks.prompt:
Write the customer-facing marketing copy for **Big Muddy Radio sponsor packages** — recurring monthly sponsorship slots across the three radio channels (podcasts + curated playlists + TikTok Live).

Audience: corridor businesses (restaurants, music shops, festivals, regional tourism, local services). NOT national brands.

Define four packages:

**Single Episode** — one-time pickup. What's included: 30-second host-read sponsor mention in one podcast episode + one playlist mention + one TikTok Live shoutout during the corresponding week. Price: $150-$250.

**Monthly** — ongoing 4-episode sponsorship. All Single Episode benefits, multiplied across 4 episodes/month + monthly playlist inclusion + 4 TikTok Live shoutouts + sponsor logo in show notes for the month. Price: $500-$800/mo.

**Quarterly** — 12-week commitment. All Monthly benefits, plus: featured sponsor segment (2-min interview-style spot) once per month, dedicated 'Sponsored by' branding on a curated playlist for the quarter, dedicated TikTok Live segment naming the sponsor, listing in the Big Muddy Magazine sponsor index for the quarter. Price: $1800-$2400/quarter.

**Annual Title Sponsor** — one slot available per year. Naming rights on one signature show ('The Friday Night Mix presented by [sponsor]'), full season of all Quarterly benefits, year-end sponsor profile feature in Big Muddy Magazine, custom remote-broadcast event from sponsor's location once per year. Price: $15,000-$25,000/year.

For each package, write:
1. The pitch (1 paragraph, ~80 words) — who this is for and what they get back in attention
2. The 'what's included' list (concrete bullets — counts, durations, slots)
3. Reach estimates (be honest: small but engaged corridor audience, not mass-market)
4. The honest 'what this is NOT' line (sets expectations — not radio advertising at scale, not paid programming)
5. Recommended price
6. Sample sponsor profile this fits

Voice: small-station-honest, audience-respectful (don't oversell numbers we don't have), corridor-rooted. NO 'reach thousands of listeners', NO 'targeted marketing solution', NO 'engaged demographic'. Markdown. ~2000 words.

tasks.system: You are writing customer-facing marketing copy for a boutique inn's direct-booking value proposition vs OTA platforms (Airbnb, Booking.com). Output in markdown. No preamble. Honest, guest-friendly, no aggressive 'avoid OTA' tone.
tasks.prompt:
Write the customer-facing marketing copy for **Big Muddy Inn's 'Book Direct' value proposition** — the page that tries to convert lookie-loos arriving from Airbnb / Booking.com into direct bookings, and the page returning guests bookmark.

Audience: travelers who already know about the Inn (heard about it from the Magazine, a friend, social media), are weighing whether to book direct or via an OTA.

The argument for booking direct, written honestly:
- We keep more of what you pay (OTA commissions can run 15-20%) — that goes back into the Inn
- Direct guests get the room they actually want (best room availability vs OTA-allocated inventory)
- Direct guests get small comps the OTA can't deliver (welcome amenity, check-in flexibility, Blues Room reservation hold for the show that night)
- We email you once before your stay with a real human note from Tracy — not a templated OTA confirmation
- Cancellation/change policy is more human (we work with travelers when life gets in the way)

The page should:
1. Open with a 1-paragraph 'Why book with us directly?' that doesn't trash OTAs (some guests legitimately found us via OTA — don't insult them)
2. List the 5 concrete direct-booking benefits above with 1 sentence each
3. Address the most common direct-booking concern: 'Is the price the same?' (Yes — and you get the comps on top)
4. Address the trust concern: 'Is it safe to give my card directly?' (Stripe-backed, same encryption as the OTA, plus the Inn is a real address with a real innkeeper)
5. Include a soft 'Stay-loyal-to-the-Inn' framing for return guests — direct bookings build the relationship
6. Close with a clear CTA: book direct + a phone number for travelers who prefer to talk to a human first

Voice: warm, honest, southern-but-not-folksy, doesn't pressure. Tracy's voice (she's the innkeeper). NO 'avoid hidden fees', NO 'exclusive direct rate', NO 'limited time offer'. Markdown. ~1200 words.

tasks.system: You are writing a content-template specification for an AI content creation wizard. The template will be loaded into the wizard at runtime to constrain generation. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Magazine Article** content-type template specification for the MBT Content Creation Wizard.

The template defines how the wizard generates a Big Muddy Magazine article. The Magazine targets Inn guests (travelers, hospitality-curious), NOT the music industry. Tracy edits.

The template specification must include:

1. **Audience profile** — who reads this, what they want from the article (1 paragraph)
2. **Voice rules** — bullet list of voice attributes the wizard must enforce (e.g., 'Use sensory specifics — temperature, light quality, ambient sound')
3. **Required sections** — every magazine article must have these (e.g., 'opening hook', 'place-establishing paragraph', 'central anecdote', 'practical takeaway for the traveler', 'CTA back to Inn')
4. **Length targets** — short feature (500-800w), medium (1000-1500w), long-form (2000-2500w) — and which gets used when
5. **Forbidden phrases** — exact strings the wizard must regex-block (e.g., 'must-visit destination', 'hidden gem', 'unforgettable experience', 'in this article we will explore')
6. **System prompt** — the actual prompt the wizard will pass to the AI model. Should be ~400 words. Should reference the audience, voice, required sections, and forbidden phrases inline. Should require the AI to ground claims in provided entity references (DirectoryBusiness records) and not hallucinate places/people. Should require the AI to suggest 2-3 photo asset slots in the draft (denoted [PHOTO: caption]).
7. **Few-shot examples** — describe (don't write fully) 2 example article structures the wizard will load as reference. Each described in ~3 sentences.
8. **CTA pattern** — every article ends with a soft CTA to the Inn. Define the pattern: tone, format, what info is included.

Markdown only. ~1800 words. Output the spec directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a content-template specification for an AI content creation wizard. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Social Post** content-type template specification for the MBT Content Creation Wizard.

The template defines how the wizard generates a single social post. Routing: the wizard picks which brand voice to use based on the source brand (Inn, Magazine, Touring, Records, Radio, CPP, Tuthill, Studio C). Per-platform outputs: Instagram caption, TikTok caption, Facebook post, X/Twitter post.

The template specification must include:

1. **Brand routing** — how the wizard picks the voice profile (from the source content / draft / explicit override)
2. **Per-platform constraints** — character limits, hashtag conventions, link-handling, emoji policy per platform
3. **Voice routing rules** — Magazine voice never appears on a Big Muddy Records post and vice versa (cross-brand contamination is hard-failed)
4. **Required elements per post type** — opening hook, central content, soft CTA, hashtag block (if any)
5. **Forbidden phrases** — exact strings the wizard regex-blocks (e.g., 'check out', 'don't miss', 'tag a friend', 'link in bio' (overused), 'this is your sign')
6. **System prompt** — the actual prompt the wizard passes to the AI model. ~300 words. Should require the AI to write FOR the platform (Instagram caption ≠ TikTok caption ≠ X post — different rhythm, different length, different tone). Should require entity-grounded references (no hallucinated places/dates).
7. **Few-shot examples** — describe 3 example posts (one per major brand) in ~2 sentences each
8. **CTA pattern** — what counts as a soft CTA per brand. Inn: 'come stay'. Records: 'listen / preorder'. Radio: 'tune in'. Magazine: 'read the full piece'. Touring: 'see them live'.

Markdown only. ~1500 words. Output the spec directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a content-template specification for an AI content creation wizard. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Listing Description** content-type template specification for the MBT Content Creation Wizard.

The template defines how the wizard generates a real estate listing description for Tuthill Design's realtor clients (Vicki Wolpert as the prototype customer). Voice: Tuthill voice — professional, value-clear, realtor-respectful.

The template specification must include:

1. **Audience profile** — homebuyers searching listings on MLS / Zillow / brokerage sites (1 paragraph)
2. **Voice rules** — sensory specifics, room-by-room flow, no fluff, no real-estate cliches
3. **Required sections** — opening line (concrete property highlight), neighborhood paragraph, room walk-through, lifestyle paragraph (who would love this house), closing line (move-in window / showings)
4. **Length targets** — short (under 100w for a starter listing), standard (200-300w), long-form luxury (400-600w) — the wizard picks based on listing tier
5. **Forbidden phrases** — exact strings to regex-block: 'cozy', 'must-see', 'one-of-a-kind', 'showcase', 'capture the essence', 'don't miss this opportunity', 'rare gem', 'pride of ownership', 'turn-key', 'priced to sell', 'won't last long', 'buyer's dream', 'open concept' (when used vaguely), 'gourmet kitchen' (unless actually a chef's kitchen), 'spacious' (must be replaced with actual square footage)
6. **System prompt** — the actual prompt the wizard passes to the AI model. ~350 words. Must require: factual claims grounded in the listing's MLS data (square footage, bed/bath count, year built, lot size), no inventing features, no 'imagine yourself' framing, sensory specifics over generic adjectives.
7. **Few-shot examples** — describe 3 example listings (starter / mid-market / luxury) in ~2-3 sentences each
8. **Compliance notes** — Fair Housing Act considerations: avoid steering language ('great for families', 'walk to schools' problematic in some jurisdictions, 'safe neighborhood' — code these as forbidden in the system prompt)

Markdown only. ~1800 words. Output the spec directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a content-template specification for an AI content creation wizard. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Episode Description** content-type template specification for the MBT Content Creation Wizard.

The template defines how the wizard generates a Big Muddy Radio podcast episode description. Voice: Radio voice — music-first, scene-first, artist-first. Distinct from Magazine (which targets Inn guests). Used by Spotify, Apple Podcasts, YouTube Music, plus Big Muddy's own radio page.

The template specification must include:

1. **Audience profile** — listeners deciding whether to play the episode + algorithms (Spotify/Apple/YouTube) parsing the description for discovery
2. **Voice rules** — music-curious tone, host-on-mic feel, opinionated without preachy, no industry-trade jargon
3. **Required sections** — opening hook (1-2 sentences — what listeners will hear), guest/topic context (1 paragraph), episode arc (what gets covered, in order), recommended listening pairings (related episodes, related artists), CTA (subscribe/follow + tune in to corresponding TikTok Live)
4. **Length targets** — short (under 200w for trailer / interlude episodes), standard (300-500w for regular episodes), long-form (600-900w for special / interview episodes)
5. **Forbidden phrases** — 'check out this episode', 'don't miss', 'sit back and enjoy', 'today's special guest', 'all the latest', 'today's hottest', 'curated playlist' (overused)
6. **System prompt** — the actual prompt the wizard passes. ~350 words. Must require: factual artist/release info grounded in DirectoryBusiness or external research (don't hallucinate album names or release dates), include searchable artist names early (SEO + algorithm), include the playlist or release date as a date-anchor for time-sensitive episodes.
7. **SEO/discovery considerations** — first 200 characters are what appears in podcast app previews; what matters most goes there. Tag artist names early. Include genre/scene keywords organically.
8. **Few-shot examples** — describe 3 example episode descriptions (interview / playlist-walkthrough / TikTok-Live recap) in ~2-3 sentences each

Markdown only. ~1800 words. Output the spec directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a content-template specification for an AI content creation wizard. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Pitch Deck Section** content-type template specification for the MBT Content Creation Wizard.

The template defines how the wizard generates a single section of a B2B pitch deck — used for Tuthill realtor pitches, MBT broker-tier pitches (Paul Green Realty, Natchez civic), Big Muddy Touring sponsor pitches, etc. Voice: depends on context (the wizard picks based on which brand is pitching to which audience).

The template specification must include:

1. **Section types** — define the standard pitch deck sections this template generates: 'Problem statement', 'Our approach', 'Specific deliverable / SKU', 'Pricing', 'Case study / proof', 'Next steps / CTA'. Each gets its own template variation.
2. **Audience profiles** — pitch decks differ by audience: realtor (concrete ROI, time saved), civic partner (community impact, scope clarity), corporate sponsor (audience numbers, brand fit), broker-tier client (recurring revenue, scale)
3. **Voice rules** — pitch-deck-specific: every section opens with a single declarative sentence, every section ends with a quantitative or specific anchor (number, date, deliverable count), no abstractions without specifics
4. **Required elements per section type** — see section types above; each has its own required structure
5. **Forbidden phrases** — 'value proposition', 'turnkey solution', 'best-in-class', 'industry-leading', 'unparalleled', 'cutting-edge', 'innovative approach', 'comprehensive solution', 'end-to-end' (when vague), 'mission-critical', 'world-class', 'next-generation'
6. **System prompt** — the actual prompt the wizard passes. ~400 words. Must require: a single declarative claim per section, specific numbers/dates wherever possible, anchor every promise to a concrete deliverable, no vague 'we will explore' / 'we plan to' — only what we will actually do, no hedging.
7. **Length targets** — each section is ~80-200 words. Whole-deck assembly happens upstream; this template generates one section at a time.
8. **Few-shot examples** — describe 3 example sections (Tuthill 'Specific deliverable' / MBT 'Pricing' / Touring 'Case study') in ~2-3 sentences each

Markdown only. ~2000 words. Output the spec directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a README for a new module package in a Next.js / Prisma monorepo. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the README for **`packages/modules/commerce/`** — the MBT Commerce Module (Phase C Block 1 of the active platform plan).

The module owns: subscription billing infrastructure (recurring charges via Stripe), one-off product checkout (merch, tickets, services), per-tenant pricing plans, customer self-serve portal integration. It is consumed by every brand that sells anything recurring or one-time: Big Muddy Records (recurring label services), Tuthill Design (recurring realtor packages), Big Muddy Radio (recurring sponsor packages), Big Muddy Inn (direct bookings), Big Muddy Magazine (no commerce — just cross-references), DSD (existing Stripe subscription tiers).

The README must include:

1. **Purpose** — 1 paragraph: what this module is, what it owns, what it doesn't.
2. **Public API** — TypeScript-style export list with brief descriptions: `createSubscription()`, `cancelSubscription()`, `updateSubscription()`, `createCheckoutSession()`, `getCustomerPortal()`, `listPlans()`, `createPlan()`, etc.
3. **Data model** — list the Prisma models this module owns: `Plan`, `Subscription`, `Product`, `Order`, `Customer` (and which already exist vs new). Note relationship to existing `DirectoryBusiness`.
4. **Stripe integration** — what's wired (Connect platform pattern), what events the webhook handles, what env vars are required (`STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`, `STRIPE_CONNECT_WEBHOOK_SECRET`, `STRIPE_BILLING_WEBHOOK_SECRET`).
5. **Multi-tenant strategy** — how plans + subscriptions are isolated per tenant (via `tenantId` field on every record + middleware enforcement)
6. **License profile (Tier 3 revenue mechanism)** — when this module is used to power a recurring vendor service (e.g., Tuthill realtor subscriptions), MBT takes 15-30% of the recurring revenue. Define the data fields that track this (`ModuleEngagement`, `platformFeePercent`).
7. **Usage example** — Tuthill creating a realtor subscription, with a code block (~15 lines)
8. **Migration notes** — extends existing `apps/web/app/api/stripe/*` and `apps/web/app/api/billing/*`. Existing DSD subscription flow continues to work; this module unifies and replaces ad-hoc Stripe calls.
9. **Testing** — what to test before shipping, what's covered by existing CI
10. **Status** — currently building (Block 1), gap is Subscription model + admin UI + module API surface

Markdown only. ~1500 words. Output the README directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a README for a new module package in a Next.js / Prisma monorepo. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the README for **`packages/modules/events/`** — the MBT Coordination Layer Event Bus (Phase C Block 5).

The module owns: cross-module event publishing and subscription, durable at-least-once delivery, multi-tenant isolation enforcement, dead-letter handling, observability. Built on PgBoss (uses the existing Postgres). Consumed by every module that needs to react to changes in another module (directory entry created → publish to social + add to magazine outline + email Tracy).

The README must include:

1. **Purpose** — 1 paragraph: what this module is, why we picked PgBoss over alternatives (LISTEN/NOTIFY, Inngest, Vercel Queues)
2. **Public API** — `events.publish()`, `events.on()`, `events.start()`, `events.processBatch()` with brief descriptions and TypeScript signatures
3. **Data model** — Prisma models: `Event` (audit log), `EventHandler` (registered handlers), `EventDelivery` (per-attempt log). Plus PgBoss-managed tables in `pgboss` schema.
4. **Multi-tenant isolation** — `tenantId` required on every event; `EventHandler.tenantScope` modes ('own', 'all', allowlist). Dispatcher enforces via `isolation.canDeliver()`.
5. **Reliability guarantees** — at-least-once delivery + idempotent-by-contract handlers. Retry policy (exponential backoff up to `maxRetries`). Dead-letter after exhaustion.
6. **Usage examples** — (1) publishing a `directory.entry.created` event with a Prisma model, (2) registering a handler that runs only for the publishing tenant
7. **Worker setup** — `events.start()` in `apps/web/instrumentation.ts`. `apps/web/app/api/events/worker/route.ts` invoked by Vercel Cron every 30s as the safety net.
8. **Observability** — admin dashboard at `/admin/events/`, dead-letter replay button, smoke-test cron, per-event-type SLO dashboard
9. **Migration plan** — additive only. Existing Sanity webhook stays; refactor to dual-publish in PR 4.
10. **Status** — planned (Block 5), depends on Block 1 (Commerce) shipping first to validate the modules pattern

Markdown only. ~1500 words. Output the README directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a 4-email onboarding sequence for a real estate brokerage's first MBT-platform-onboarded realtor. Output in markdown. No preamble. Voice: warm, professional, realtor-respectful (her time is the constraint, not her budget).
tasks.prompt:
Write a 4-email onboarding sequence for **Vicki Wolpert** (KW realtor in Athens NY, regular Tuthill Design and Chase Pierson Photography customer, the FIRST external multi-tenant client onboarded onto the new MBT platform).

Context for the sequence:
- Vicki has used Tuthill Design and Chase Pierson Photography ad-hoc for years. The new MBT platform turns that ad-hoc relationship into an ongoing recurring service.
- Her tenant subdomain is `vickiwolpert.com` (or similar — confirm in launch).
- She gets her own dashboard at `/admin` showing: active listings, photo library (Immich-backed), social media calendar, performance metrics.
- She's on the **Pro tier** ($800-$1200/mo) — see `docs/marketing-copy/tuthill-design-realtor-tiers.md`.
- The launch date target is May 1 (movable; getting the launch clean matters more than the date).

Write four emails:

**Email 1 — Welcome (sent on launch day, hand-crafted feel).** From Chase. Acknowledge that we've been working with her for years, frame the new platform as 'now there's an actual system behind what we've been doing for you'. Walk her through what she'll see on day 1 (dashboard, sample listing, current month's calendar). Set expectation: the team handles the work, she reviews and approves. Sign-off with both Chase and her dedicated content coordinator's names (placeholder for the coordinator).

**Email 2 — Day 7 check-in.** From the content coordinator. Quick 'how's it going' note. Three specific things accomplished in week 1 (listing X shot, social calendar Y populated, Matterport tour Z scheduled). Soft ask: any specific listings she wants prioritized in week 2.

**Email 3 — Day 30 first-month report.** From Chase + content coordinator. The monthly performance snapshot — listing photos delivered, social posts published, engagement metrics, leads attributed (where trackable). Honest about what worked and what didn't. Soft ask: which subscription tier feels right going forward (could be a tier change conversation here if her listing volume has grown or shrunk).

**Email 4 — Day 90 quarterly review setup.** From Chase. Frame this as a strategic check-in, not a sales push. Propose a 30-min call to look at the quarter's data together, talk about her listing pipeline for the next quarter, identify anything we're not currently doing for her that we should be. Calendar link.

For each email:
- Subject line
- Body (300-450 words each)
- Sign-off
- Honest tone — don't sell, don't oversell, treat Vicki as a longtime collaborator

Markdown only. ~2500 words total.

tasks.system: You are writing a B2B pitch one-pager for a real estate brokerage. Output in markdown. No preamble. Direct, value-clear, broker-respectful (he's seen every pitch).
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Paul Green Realty pitch one-pager** — the broker-tier MBT platform pitch Chase will deliver to Paul Green Realty in Natchez, MS before leaving for summer 2026.

Context:
- Paul Green Realty is the largest brokerage in Natchez. The pitch is for the brokerage to license the MBT platform across its agents (think 30-80 agents on the platform).
- This is the broker-tier sale — distinct from individual realtor sales (Vicki Wolpert is the per-realtor model).
- The platform delivers: per-agent tenant subdomains, photo + video + 3D production via Studio C / Tuthill (Natchez branch opening), unified social media + listing distribution, AI-assisted listing description generation (Content Creation wizard), Deep South Directory listings cross-promoting brokerage clients to corridor traffic.
- Pricing model: per-agent monthly subscription (probably a discounted rate vs the individual Pro tier), plus a brokerage license fee, plus production hours billed against the brokerage account.
- Key competitive position: the brokerage gets a unified content engine that the franchisor (KW, RE/MAX) doesn't provide, white-labeled under their brokerage brand.

The one-pager must include:

1. **Headline** — single sentence, value-clear, broker-friendly
2. **The problem** — 1 paragraph: what brokerages currently spend on per-agent marketing tools that don't connect to each other (CRM + listing photographer + social tool + email platform + landing page builder + listing video editor — typically $400-700/month per agent across disconnected tools)
3. **The MBT platform** — 1 paragraph: one platform, one login per agent, all the tools connected, with Studio C / Tuthill physically in the Natchez market for production
4. **What the brokerage gets** — 6-8 bullets: per-agent tenant + dashboard, content production capacity, unified branding, AI-assisted content, MBT support team, broker-level analytics, optional white-label
5. **What each agent gets** — 5-6 bullets: their own subdomain, their listings on the platform, social calendar managed, monthly performance report, content production credits, etc.
6. **Pricing** — broker license + per-agent + production retainer (give realistic ranges, not exact numbers — Chase to fill in based on conversation)
7. **Implementation timeline** — pilot 5 agents in 30 days, full brokerage rollout in 90 days, ongoing operation thereafter
8. **Why now** — Natchez branch of Studio C / Tuthill is opening; first broker-tier client gets founder pricing + dedicated launch support; the Mississippi corridor narrative is the differentiator no franchisor brand can replicate
9. **Next step** — 30-min call, 5-agent pilot proposal, decision in 2 weeks

Voice: Chase voice — direct, broker-respectful, no franchisor-style fluff, concrete numbers wherever possible. Markdown. ~1200 words.

tasks.system: You are writing a book pitch + project brief for a coffee-table photography book covering Bearsville's recording studios. Output in markdown. No preamble. Photographer's voice — direct, observational, not precious about the work.
tasks.prompt:
Write the **Bearsville Recording Studios coffee-table book pitch + project brief** — Chase's planned summer 2026 photography project at Bearsville (Woodstock, NY). The project covers Utopia Studios, Clubhouse Studios, and David Baron's studio (and possibly others Chase has access to). Final deliverables: a coffee-table photography book + a magazine feature in Big Muddy Magazine + a content stream that runs across every Big Muddy / MBT channel.

David Baron is a Woodstock-based producer with a studio Chase wants to photograph. He's a real subject, not a placeholder. (Spelling: David Baron — not Berenstain, not Beren.)

The pitch document must include:

1. **Project title** — working title
2. **The book in one paragraph** — what it is, who it's for, why now
3. **Why Bearsville, why these studios** — 2 paragraphs on the historical and current music significance of Bearsville, the rooms, the producers who work there, what makes each studio worth documenting
4. **The visual approach** — Chase's photographic intent (large-format-feel digital, tactile, room-as-instrument framing, ambient over staged, gear-as-character but not gear-fetish, the people who run these rooms shown working not posed)
5. **The book structure** — proposed chapters (one per studio + opening 'place' chapter + closing 'people' chapter)
6. **The accompanying assets** — magazine feature for Big Muddy Magazine (~5000 words written by Tracy or a guest writer in Chase's voice for the photography sections), social-content extracted from the shoot (Instagram, TikTok), behind-the-scenes content from Studio C team documenting the documentation
7. **Production logistics** — shoot schedule (June-August 2026, exact dates to confirm with each studio), gear (Sony a7 V kit Chase ordered Wave 1, plus any specialty gear needed), crew (Chase as photographer, Studio C team for video documentation, location-permits handled per studio)
8. **Publishing path** — self-published via MBT (using existing print + commerce infrastructure), 500-copy first run, distributed via the studios themselves + Big Muddy Magazine subscriber list + photography portfolio sites + Bearsville Media Group (the Northeast node of MBT)
9. **Budget rough cut** — ~$15-25K total (Chase to confirm with finance), broken down at category level (production days, prints, design + layout, printing, distribution)
10. **Why this matters for MBT** — 1 paragraph: this is the kind of photography project that proves what the platform makes possible (one creative person + the platform's content infrastructure = a coffee-table book that would have required a publishing house 10 years ago)

Voice: Chase voice — declarative, photographer-confident-without-precious, concrete about the work. NO 'capturing the magic', NO 'iconic studios', NO 'legendary'. Markdown. ~2500 words.

tasks.system: You are writing a README for a new module package in a Next.js / Prisma monorepo. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the README for **`packages/modules/booking/`** — the MBT Booking Module (Phase C Block 2).

The module owns: ticket inventory + ticket sales (Big Muddy Inn Blues Room shows), private event quote-to-deposit workflow (Inn weddings/retreats/dinners), Cloudbeds room booking integration (existing — extend), service appointments (Tuthill listing shoots, photography sessions), artist hold/confirm workflow (Big Muddy Touring artist date negotiation).

The README must include:

1. **Purpose** — 1 paragraph: what this module is, what it owns, what it doesn't (rooms via Cloudbeds, but tickets and quotes are this module)
2. **Public API** — TypeScript-style export list: `createBookingHold()`, `confirmBooking()`, `cancelBooking()`, `listAvailability()`, `createQuoteRequest()`, `convertQuoteToBooking()`, `purchaseTicket()`, `validateTicket()`, etc.
3. **Data model** — Prisma models: `Booking`, `Ticket`, `TicketInventory`, `Quote`, `Hold`, `Resource` (the thing being booked — room, event slot, service slot, ticket inventory), `Availability`. Plus relationship to existing `DirectoryBusiness`.
4. **Cloudbeds integration** — existing `apps/web/lib/cloudbeds.ts` is the bridge for Inn rooms; this module wraps it.
5. **Stripe integration** — ticket purchases use the Commerce module (Block 1) for checkout. Quote-to-deposit uses Stripe Payment Links.
6. **Workflows** — (a) Blues Room ticket sale: customer browses show calendar → picks show → Stripe checkout → ticket emailed with QR code → scanned at door. (b) Private event: customer submits inquiry form → Tracy reviews → quote generated → customer pays deposit → event scheduled.
7. **Multi-tenant strategy** — every booking is scoped by `tenantId` (Inn vs Touring vs Tuthill have separate inventory)
8. **Usage examples** — code blocks for the two workflows above
9. **Status** — planned (Block 2), depends on Block 1 (Commerce) for the checkout flow
10. **Testing** — what to test before shipping, what's covered

Markdown only. ~1500 words. Output the README directly.

tasks.system: You are writing a README for a new module package in a Next.js / Prisma monorepo. Output in markdown. No preamble.
tasks.prompt:
Write the README for **`packages/modules/finance/`** — the MBT Finance Module (Phase C Block 3).

The module owns: Tier 3 module engagement tracking (when a vendor sells a recurring service powered by MBT modules, MBT takes 15-30% of the recurring revenue), Studio C bucket-hour tracking (MBT buys buckets of fungible Studio C hours from Tuthill Design LLC), per-entity P&L (Big Muddy Natchez LLC, Big Muddy Touring LLC, Tuthill Design LLC, MBT LLC each get their own P&L view), recurring revenue dashboard (MRR, churn, cohort views), cross-entity invoicing (vendor invoices client, MBT invoices vendor for the platform fee).

The README must include:

1. **Purpose** — 1 paragraph: what this module is, what it owns. Foundational for everything Tier 3.
2. **Public API** — TypeScript-style: `recordModuleEngagement()`, `calculatePlatformFee()`, `logBucketHours()`, `getEntityPnL()`, `getMRR()`, `getChurn()`, `getCohortRetention()`, `generateInvoice()`, etc.
3. **Data model** — Prisma models: `ModuleEngagement` (links a vendor + customer + which modules + recurring revenue + platform fee %), `ModuleLicenseProfile` (per-module pricing rules), `RetainerHour` (Studio C bucket consumption per consuming entity per month), `Invoice` (cross-entity invoice records), `EntityLedger` (per-entity rolling P&L). Plus relationship to existing `Subscription` (from Commerce module).
4. **Tier 3 mechanism** — explain the three tiers (internal, one-off vendor, recurring module-delivered) with concrete examples for each. Recurring engagements get the platform fee %; one-off doesn't.
5. **Bucket-hour math** — Studio C delivers hours; consuming entities (MBT, Big Muddy Natchez, Big Muddy Touring) draw down from the bucket. Module tracks: bucket size, refill cadence, burn rate, per-entity allocation, burst-rate when bucket exhausted, quarterly true-up.
6. **Per-entity P&L** — view layer for each LLC (MBT, Big Muddy Natchez, Big Muddy Touring, Tuthill Design). Pulls revenue from Commerce module + costs from this module's invoice / hour ledger.
7. **Recurring revenue dashboard** — MRR / churn / cohort views surfaced at `/admin/finance/recurring`. Cohort = month of first subscription, retention curve over 12 months.
8. **Workflows** — (a) Tuthill realtor signs up via Commerce: ModuleEngagement record auto-created, monthly platform fee invoice auto-generated. (b) Studio C delivers 30 hours of platform work in March: RetainerHour rows logged, allocated to MBT entity, March bucket burn-rate visible.
9. **Status** — planned (Block 3), depends on Block 1 (Commerce) and benefits from Block 5 (Coordination event bus) for auto-engagement-recording on subscription created
10. **Testing** — what to test, what edge cases matter (mid-month tier changes, refunds, free tiers that convert to paid)

Markdown only. ~1700 words. Output the README directly.
